paper_title: Lake Sampling and Diatom Community Analysis

methods.description: This method describes the field procedures for sampling lakes, including bathymetry measurements, logger installation, physical variable measurements, and collection of water, planktic diatom, periphytic diatom, and sediment samples. It also covers the subsequent laboratory processing of these samples for chemical analysis and diatom identification.

steps.description: The 43 lakes were sampled twice during the ice-free period: once between June and mid-August and once between August and November. Thirty-six lakes were investigated in 2016 and seven in 2017.
steps.searchable_summary: Plan and execute lake sampling campaigns

steps.description: On the first sampling date, lake bathymetry was determined with an echo sounder (Lawrence HDS8, Oslo, Norway)
steps.searchable_summary: Measure lake bathymetry using echo sounder

steps.description: subsequently, a buoy was installed, attached by rope to a stone at the deepest point of each lake.
steps.searchable_summary: Install buoy at deepest point of lake

steps.description: Temperature loggers (Onset Pendant UA-001-64 HOBO, Bourne, USA) were mounted on the rope 0.5 m above the ground and 0.5 m below the water surface.
steps.searchable_summary: Mount temperature loggers on rope at specified depths

steps.description: During most of the ice-free period, the loggers in the lakes were exposed to assess the temperature regime and the lake's mixing type.
steps.searchable_summary: Deploy temperature loggers for long-term monitoring

steps.description: On both sampling dates, physical variables (temperature, oxygen saturation, pH, and electrical conductivity at 25 °C) were measured with a multiprobe (WTW 350, Weilheim, Germany) in one-metre steps above the deepest point of each lake.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure vertical profiles of physical variables using multiprobe

steps.description: After measuring the Secchi depth, 0.5 L of a mixed water sample-comprising the euphotic zone (CEN, 2015)-was collected with a hose sampler.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure Secchi depth and collect mixed water sample from euphotic zone

steps.description: One half of the water sample was filtered (0.45 µm) on-site and stored at 4 °C together with the unfiltered rest until further processing at the laboratory.
steps.searchable_summary: Filter half of water sample on-site and store filtered and unfiltered portions

steps.description: Another litre of water was taken from the euphotic zone with the hose sampler and preserved with Lugol's solution to analyse planktic diatom communities (Throndsen, 1978). Planktic samples were taken from every lake on the first sampling date.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect 1 L water sample from euphotic zone and preserve for planktic diatom analysis
outputs: 1 L water sample preserved with Lugol's solution

steps.description: Periphytic diatom assemblages were assessed by scraping off the diatom communities of five stones at depths of between 20 and 50 cm in the northern littoral zone of each lake with a single-use toothbrush (DIN, 2014). At nine sites there were no stones present in the littoral zone, so no samples were taken there to maintain consistency of the substratum.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect periphytic diatom samples by scraping stones in littoral zone

steps.description: On the second sampling date, sediment cores were taken from the deepest point of each lake with a gravity corer (Uwitec, Mondsee, Austria) to assess the sedimentary diatom communities (mostly composed of dead individuals) from all 43 lakes according to Küfner et al. (2020a, b, c).
steps.searchable_summary: Collect sediment cores from deepest point using gravity corer

steps.description: Hence, three diatom assemblages could be sampled: planktic diatoms from the open water, periphytic diatoms from the northern littoral zone All chemical analyses were carried out at the laboratory of the Limnological Research Station Iffeldorf at the Technical University of Munich, Germany.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare for laboratory chemical analysis

steps.description: Standard colorimetric methods using a Hitachi 150-200 photometer (Chiyoda, Japan) were applied to determine the concentrations of total phosphorus (Murphy & Riley, 1962), nitrate-N (Navone, 1964), ammonia-N (DIN, 1983 and silica.
steps.searchable_summary: Determine nutrient concentrations (TP, NO3-N, NH3-N, Si) using colorimetric methods

steps.description: The concentrations of major ions (calcium, magnesium and sodium) were analysed using a cation chromatograph (Thermo Scientific, ICS-1100, Waltham, USA).
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze major ion concentrations (Ca, Mg, Na) using cation chromatography

steps.description: Planktic diatom samples (1 L) were filtered with 0.45-µm syringe filters and the residue on the filters was further processed (Nixdorf et al., 2014).
steps.searchable_summary: Filter planktic diatom samples using syringe filters

steps.description: The uppermost centimetre of each sediment core was used to assess the sedimentary diatom assemblages present.
steps.searchable_summary: Subsample uppermost centimeter of sediment cores

steps.description: The residue on the filters of the planktic samples, the sediment samples and the littoral samples were processed in the same way: preparation of the diatoms was carried out according to van der Werff and Macan (1955).
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare diatom samples (planktic, sedimentary, periphytic) using standard method (van der Werff and Macan 1955)

steps.description: A total of 500 valves were identified in each case using a Leica DNM microscope (Wetzlar, Germany) at ×1000 magnification to analyse the composition of the diatom samples.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify and count 500 diatom valves per sample using microscopy

steps.description: Eleven of the 43 planktic samples were excluded from the data analysis because they contained an insufficient number of valves.
steps.searchable_summary: Exclude samples with insufficient diatom valve counts

steps.description: Taxa were counted at the species level and where possible, at the subspecies level. Individuals that could not be identified were given working names. Standard literature was used for identification (Krammer, 2000(Krammer, , 2002Krammer & Lange-Bertalot, 1991a, b, 1997aLange-Bertalot, 2001;Lange-Bertalot et al., 2017).
steps.searchable_summary: Identify diatom taxa to species/subspecies level using standard literature

methods.description: This method describes the statistical and ecological analyses performed on the collected environmental and diatom community data, including diversity calculations, regression modeling, distance-based analyses, variation partitioning, and null model analysis.

steps.description: To select the environmental variables used in distance decay analysis and variation partitioning, separate redundancy analysis (RDA) models for sedimentary, littoral and planktic diatom assemblages were computed based on Hellinger-transformed assemblage data (Legendre & Gallagher, 2001). Only variables with variance inflation factors less than five for all data sets were included in the RDAs. VIF's were calculated and variables were selected with the "vifstep" function in the "usdm" R package (Naimi, 2017).
steps.searchable_summary: Select environmental variables based on Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) using RDA and vifstep function

steps.description: All variables except for altitude and pH were log transformed and the full data set was scaled before RDA using the "scale" function in "vegan".
steps.searchable_summary: Log transform and scale environmental variables

steps.description: Pearson correlations were calculated for these variables, and P-values were Bonferroni-Holm corrected.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate Pearson correlations and apply Bonferroni-Holm correction

steps.description: Based on the results, correlation plots were produced using the "corrplot" function (Wei & Simko, 2017).
steps.searchable_summary: Generate correlation plots

steps.description: For each sample of the three assemblages, α-diversity was computed as species richness and Shannon diversity.
steps.searchable_summary: Compute alpha diversity (species richness, Shannon) for each sample

steps.description: Differences in mean α-diversity amongst the assemblages were assessed with the Wilcoxon Test as diversities were not distributed normally (Wilcoxon, 1945).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Wilcoxon test to compare alpha diversity among assemblages

steps.description: Relations between environmental variables and α-diversity measures were investigated using linear regression models and R 2 -values were calculated for significant models.
steps.searchable_summary: Fit linear regression models between environmental variables and alpha diversity

steps.description: The mean and pairwise β-diversity of diatom communities was calculated for sedimentary, littoral and planktic assemblages for abundance and incidence data, based on the method proposed by Baselga and Orme (2012).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate mean and pairwise beta diversity (abundance and incidence) using Baselga method

steps.description: For the abundance data, the multiplesite Bray-Curtis dissimilarity (β tot ) was computed and then partitioned into its "abundance balanced variation" (β bal ) and "abundance gradients" (β gra ) components (Baselga, 2017).
steps.searchable_summary: Compute and partition multisite Bray-Curtis dissimilarity (beta_tot) into beta_bal and beta_gra

steps.description: For incidence data, β-diversity was calculated as a Simpson diversity (β sim ) and then partitioned into its component for turnover (representing the Sorensen dissimilarity which measures species replacement) (β sor ) and nestedness (representing the nestedness component of the Sorensen dissimilarity, reflecting species loss) (β sne ) following Baselga (2010).
steps.searchable_summary: Compute and partition Simpson beta diversity (beta_sim) into turnover (beta_sor) and nestedness (beta_sne)

steps.description: The contribution to abundance and incidence-based β-diversity (SCBD) of each species was assessed according to Legendre and De Caceres (2013) using Hellinger-transformed data.
steps.searchable_summary: Assess species contribution to beta diversity (SCBD) using Hellinger-transformed data

steps.description: The relationship between SCBD-values and species abundance and occurrence was modelled using polynomial regression models.
steps.searchable_summary: Fit polynomial regression models for SCBD vs species abundance/occurrence

steps.description: Only variables that were identified by forward selection using the "ordiR2step" function in "vegan" (P < 0.05) were incorporated into the final environmental distance matrix (Blanchet et al., 2008).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform forward selection on environmental variables using RDA and ordiR2step

steps.description: The altitudinal distance matrix used in distance decay analysis and variation partitioning was calculated based on Euclidean distances between sites.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate altitudinal distance matrix using Euclidean distance

steps.description: Distance-based Moran's eigenvector maps (db MEM) based on the geographical coordinates of the lakes were used in variation partitioning (Dray et al., 2006) to explore spatial effects. Eigenvectors were selected using the "mem.select" function in the "ade4" R package.
steps.searchable_summary: Generate and select distance-based Moran's eigenvector maps (dbMEM) for spatial effects

steps.description: This function runs a forward selection based on R 2 -values after a global test of significance (Bauman et al., 2018). The significance of each MEM was assessed with the "moran. randtest" function that computes Moran's index and calculates P-values using random permutations.
steps.searchable_summary: Assess significance of selected dbMEM eigenvectors using permutation test

steps.description: The effect of spatial, environmental and altitudinal distance on total β-diversity (β tot , β sim ) and its components (β bal , β gra , β sor , β sne ) were investigated with partial Mantel tests (Borcard et al., 1992). Significance levels and r-values were calculated for each combination whilst controlling for the other distance matrices to obtain the exclusive effect values of the factor of interest.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform partial Mantel tests to relate beta diversity to spatial, environmental, and altitudinal distances

steps.description: Moreover, the decay of assemblage similarity with spatial, altitudinal and environmental distance was assessed by fitting negative exponential functions using the "decay.model" function in the "beta part" package (Baselga & Orme, 2012). This method provides additional information compared to Mantel tests through the shape of the regression line (Dray et al., 2020).
steps.searchable_summary: Model distance decay of assemblage similarity using negative exponential functions

steps.description: Distance decay analysis of the abundance and incidence data were complemented with variation partitioning to investigate the proportion of variance explained by environmental and spatial variables using Euclidean distances (Borcard et al., 1992).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform variation partitioning to assess variance explained by environmental and spatial variables

steps.description: To measure the relative strength of stochastic versus deterministic processes, a null model approach was applied using the "tNST" function in the "NST" R package (Ning et al., 2019). Null models were calculated for all three diatom data sets and abundance data using the Bray-Curtis distance and incidence data using the Jaccard index. This function produces results ranging from 0 to 100, with values below 50, indicating a prevalence of deterministic processes and values above 50, denoting stochasticity.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply null model approach (tNST) to assess stochastic vs deterministic processes

steps.description: The results for the data sets were compared by ANOVA, PAN-OVA and PERMANOVA with 1000 resampling runs.
steps.searchable_summary: Compare null model results across datasets using ANOVA, PAN-OVA, and PERMANOVA

steps.description: All the analyses were computed using version 3.6.6 of the "R" free statistics software, except for tNST analysis, which was conducted using version 4.1.3 (R Core Team, 2013). Data curation was facilitated using the "dplyr" package (Wickham & François, 2014).
steps.searchable_summary: Note software versions and packages used
